preamble: The voiceless palatal plosive /c/ can undergo a historical phonetic transformation to the voiceless dental fricative /θ/. This shift, observed in certain language families, represents a fascinating interplay of articulatory changes. It involves both a 'lenition' process, where a complete stop sound becomes a less obstructed fricative, and a 'fronting' of the articulation point, moving from the hard palate towards the teeth.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (plosive to fricative), Fronting (palatal to dental)

examples.note: Proto-Salish *c developed into /θ/ in Northern Straits Salish, including the Saanich dialect.

sources: Reisinger, D. K. E., & Mellesmoen, G. (eds.). (2019). A Fricative-First Path from Proto-Salish *c to /s/ and /θ/ in Central Salish. Papers for the International Conference on Salish and Neighbouring Languages 54. Vancouver: UBCWPL.
